SBC.title: SBC (Subband Codec)
SBC.summary: The basic, always-supported Bluetooth audio codec.
paragraphs: SBC is the standard, mandatory codec that every Bluetooth audio device on Earth supports. If nothing else can be negotiated, devices fall back to SBC.
It throws away a noticeable amount of detail to fit through the narrow Bluetooth pipe, so quiet background sounds and high-frequency content (cymbals, breath, room reverb) often sound smoothed-over.
Stock Windows only implements SBC for Bluetooth audio. To get anything better you need a 3rd-party driver such as Alternative A2DP Driver.

AAC.title: AAC
AAC.summary: Higher-quality codec mainly used by Apple devices.
paragraphs: AAC (Advanced Audio Coding) is the same family used by Apple Music and YouTube. Over Bluetooth it caps around 256 kbps but is more efficient than SBC bit-for-bit.
On iPhone/Mac it sounds great; on Windows and Android the encoder is often weaker and SBC at high quality can actually beat it.

aptX.title: aptX
aptX.summary: Qualcomm's low-latency codec, common on Android phones.
paragraphs: aptX is a Qualcomm codec built into most Snapdragon phones and many Bluetooth headphones. It has lower latency than SBC, which matters for video.
Quality is similar to a well-tuned SBC. Not a huge step up by itself.

aptX HD.title: aptX HD
paragraphs: aptX HD nearly doubles the data rate of regular aptX and supports 24-bit/48 kHz audio. A genuine quality bump if your headphones support it.
Less common on earbuds; more often found on over-ear cans and Hi-Fi-focused gear.

LDAC.title: LDAC
LDAC.summary: Sony's hi-res codec sending up to 3x more data than SBC.
paragraphs: LDAC is Sony's wireless audio codec, built into Android since 8.0. It can stream up to 990 kbps over Bluetooth - roughly 3x what SBC manages.
More data means cymbals stay crisp, quiet background sounds survive, and the music gets noticeably closer to listening to a CD or lossless source.

The catch: LDAC needs a strong, stable signal. If you walk away from the laptop or there's Wi-Fi interference on 2.4 GHz, it auto-drops to 660 or 330 kbps to avoid stutter. Your monitor will show when this happens.
Latency is higher than SBC/aptX (around 200ms), which is fine for music but noticeable in fast-paced gaming.

bitrate.title: Transmission speed (bitrate)
paragraphs: Bitrate is how many kilobits of music data are reaching your earbuds every second. Higher means more sound detail survives the trip through Bluetooth.
LDAC has three modes: 990 kbps (best quality), 660 kbps (balanced), 330 kbps (connection priority). The codec switches between them automatically based on signal stability.

stability.title: Connection stability
paragraphs: Windows has no API to read RSSI (signal strength in dBm) for an already-connected classic Bluetooth audio device — that's only exposed during BLE advertisement scanning, which doesn't apply to a connected earbud. So instead of faking a signal number, this counts real disconnects and codec downgrades.

'Stable' = no disconnects or codec downgrades in the last 10 minutes. 'Occasional drops' = 1-2 events. 'Unstable' = 3 or more — usually means you're at the edge of range, there's 2.4GHz interference (Wi-Fi, microwaves), or the connection is congested.
A codec downgrade (e.g. LDAC dropping from 990 to 330 kbps) is itself a sign the connection got weaker, even without a full disconnect.

latency.title: Latency
paragraphs: Latency is the delay between sound leaving your laptop and reaching your ears. Measured in milliseconds (ms).
Compressing high-quality audio takes processing time on both ends. 150-250 ms is normal for LDAC. SBC and aptX are lower (~100-150 ms). aptX LL is built specifically for low latency.
Fine for music; modern video apps auto-sync; can be noticeable in fast-paced gaming.

battery.title: Earbud battery
paragraphs: Battery percentage as reported by the earbuds over Bluetooth.

Many true-wireless earbuds only expose a single combined battery level to Windows (not separate left/right/case). Per-bud and case battery typically requires the manufacturer's mobile app or a Bluetooth LE GATT query that this device may not advertise.

sample_rate.title: Sample rate & bit depth
paragraphs: Sample rate is how many audio measurements per second (48 kHz = 48,000 samples). Bit depth is how precise each measurement is (16-bit = 65,536 levels, 24-bit = 16.7 million levels).
CD quality is 44.1 kHz / 16-bit. Hi-res starts at 48 kHz / 24-bit. LDAC supports up to 96 kHz / 24-bit.

Heads up: Windows' shared audio engine often resamples everything to its mix format (usually 48 kHz / 16-bit) before sending to Bluetooth - so even hi-res LDAC may get downsampled in software first unless you use WASAPI exclusive mode.

bluetooth.title: Bluetooth output
paragraphs: Wireless connection - convenient, no cables, but limited by codec choice and signal strength.
Quality depends entirely on which codec your device and PC agree on. Stock Windows = SBC only. With Alternative A2DP Driver = LDAC/aptX HD possible.

wired.title: Wired output
paragraphs: Direct analog signal from the headphone jack or built-in speakers, driven by the system's audio chip.
No compression, no codec, no battery to drain. The cleanest possible path - usually beats any Bluetooth codec for absolute fidelity if your headphones are good.
